ground_truth.title: Which is the best Audiobook Subscription Service?
ground_truth.author: Adrian
ground_truth.main_content:
I've written about the benefits of listening to audiobooks on this site previously, but I haven't talked about how I listen to them. In this post I'm going to talk about exactly that, as I've used several audiobook services and I thought I'd look at the differences, and offer an audiobook subscription comparison.

Amazon Audible Audiobooks was first launched in the market back in 1995, so it's well-established by now. It's a subscription model, so for a fee each month you get one credit, allowing you to listen to the audiobook of your choice. Whatever you're looking for, you'll find it here. It has everything current, bestsellers etc, which isn't always the case with the other audiobook subscription services. Lots of Agatha Christie audiobooks, always popular. And if you do use your credit and find you're still hungry for more, included with your membership is the Plus Catalogue. I enjoy the podcast exclusives.

Audible even allows you to return a book. Maybe you've taken a chance on a book, but you don't like the narrator or the title doesn't take your fancy, then as a member you can return it. This is at the discretion of Audible, and there's no point in trying to return a twelve hour audiobook after ten hours.

Spotify Audiobooks with my premium account, I have access to 15 hours a month, which works out on average of about one and a half or sometimes two books a month, depending on length. And if you run out of time, you can buy ten more hours and add them to your account - this will run on for a year. I've been impressed with the range of titles available here - anything I've wanted to listen to, I've been able to find.

Audible pricing: £7.99/month for 1 credit, £13.99/month for 2 credits. You own what you buy. Extensive range. Easy to use across all devices. Generous free trial. Spotify pricing: Included with Spotify Premium (£10.99/month as of 2025). All-in-one app for music, podcasts, and audiobooks.

Chirpbooks is competition and provides another alternative. It's run by the same team behind Bookbub. One immediate advantage is that opening an account is free. Unfortunately, it only seems to be open to US & Canada residents.

Apple Audiobooks - no subscription here, you purchase the books, so you have them to listen to any time you want to. This would be particularly useful to those of us who have favorite audiobooks and like to listen to them repeatedly.

Scribd (Everand) for a flat monthly fee you get access to a large library of ebooks and audiobooks. You stream or download for offline use, but you don't own the titles. Once your subscription ends, you lose access.

Libby Audiobooks is another audiobook service I have used for several years. The best thing about the app compared to the others is that it is free and couldn't be simpler - download the app, link it to your library card, and away you go. On the downside, it doesn't have the range that the other subscription-based models do.

Borrowbox is also a favourite of mine and is also used by many libraries, and it is also free with a great range of titles. Librovox also gets a mention, because it's free and concentrates on public domain titles - that's books that are out of copyright, so if you want to explore the classics, this might be the one for you.

Summary: So there you have it, my guide to audiobook subscription services. In answer to the question of which is the best audiobook subscription, Libby/borrow box/LibroVox are obviously the way to go. These are great alternatives to Audible.
